item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Administer kava-kava daily or as needed. It short-circuits positive feedback loops of negative emotions by numbing the nerves that react to the presence of cortisol in the bloodstream. It won't stop him from getting excited, but it'll prevent existing excitement from making him more excited. As a nice side-effect, it also helps with depression and anxiety.

It shouldn't exacerbate the dizziness, but it may induce the perception of dizziness at lower RPMs than usual. (Basically those same nerve endings adjacent to blood vessels that normally react with physical pain in response to the presence of cortisol, also react to changes in blood flow caused by gravity to tell the brain which way is up. It doesn't affect the autonomic nervous system though, that's the inner ear.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: One tiny change, a negative feedback loop means that the head would slow the spinning given the emotional response. I changed it to positive feedback loop, reflecting the distress causing the man's head to spin further.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Chiming in here to agree with TL333s regarding the dialogue. Backstory delivered like this is usually grating to read for me unless the recounted events are particularly interesting or the character is sufficiently entertaining and I didn't get enough of either here.

To ease up on this, I would suggest giving some more back-and forth, maybe even add a third party to the interview if it'd allow for a less vanilla Q/A exchange. Your Punkz is a shining example of using dialogue as a vehicle for story, due in part to allowing differing personalities to bounce off one another while they espoused.

It may also be worth considering the context itself: is there a better way to frame the story to take some of the burden of explanation off the dialogue? Could some of the information be presented in the history to open up room for something more emotive or action-packed in their recollection?

ETA: Another way of putting it: I think that, as it stands now, the whole thing comes off as 'too much all at once'. For the most part, each section of his story (creation, trafficking, meeting with circus folk, daring escape) could very well stand to be an entire conversation on its own. Telling it in such broad strokes takes away from pacing by rushing through plot points, hampering the sense of narrative progression. It's less 'telling a story' than it is giving the sparknotes of one.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Either put the microphone on a ceiling fan, or spin on an office chair.
Take regulated breaks in the latter case.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Luna is Best girl. I called it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I love that mental image of the guy waking up in the backalley parasurgeon's office.

Ethan: "Why is the room spinning!? What the hell did you do to me!?"
First Guy: »Why is his head spinning!? What the hell did you do to him Dimitri!?«
Dimitri: »These things happen.«
Ethan: "FIX THIS!"
First Guy: »FIX HIM!«
Dimitri: »Perhaps if we hold his head still…«
Sound of heart monitor alarm going off
Dimitri: »Okay, no one do that anymore.«
sound of Dimitri being dopeslapped

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Though I don't entirely agree that SCP-3619 using the term 'ancient spirits' is out-of-character, I have changed it nonetheless. I've also edited the line you highlighted.

As for the first problem you brought up, I'm not sure how to fix that. I'm principally a tale writer, and though I would like to think I've improved at writing SCPs since I first started, the narrative element always ends up coming out rather tale-like.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Aaw, poor Victor. I'm sure he really thought he was helping, the Circus is one of the better places an anomalous human could end up.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If any skip ever needed an embedded soundtrack it's this one. https://youtu.be/PGNiXGX2nLU

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm impressed at how you were able to make a simple humanoid anomaly so effective. That said, a couple things are holding me back from an upvote. His dialogue in the interview comes in big, expository blocks, and it often feels like he's narrating a story, like, written narration, not spoken, not from memory.

Further study into SCP-3619's anatomy is severely hampered by the fact that non-euclidean spaces are, by definition, incomprehensible to human beings.

Also, this doesn't quite seem right. I mean, even putting aside "the Foundation contains a lot of non-Euclidean anomalies", it would just be simpler to cut it off at "hampered" (with a "has been", of course), there's no need to explain why.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like this, the anomaly is low-key and weird and the interview has a nice sense of world building to it.
My only issue with it is, as Tempered Knight mentioned above, this line:

and it unleashed dozens of ancient spirits

Feels super awkward.

Something like "and a bunch of ghosts shot out of it" or something would work much better.
In any case, +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I giggled at the Doppler effect idea.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really love this one. He's just a guy who's head is literally spinning and he doesn't like it very much. It's sort of a mental image pun. It doesn't try to do any more than it needs to.

I do kinda wish there was a way to pull off an audio log of his interview so you could get the gag of the doppler effect distorting his speech, that would be amazing :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is great, Chandra! Nitpick:

I smashed a vase, just to use its fragments as a weapon, and it unleashed dozens of ancient spirits who started tearing up the place.

This just feels clunky and unnatural to me. Try:

I smashed a vase, and ghosts or something blasted out of it. While they were tearing up the place…

Overall, pretty good article! I tend to enjoy these simple anomalies, because it really solidifies the Foundationverse. Not everything needs to have a sinister purpose to it.

item.title: Author Post
item.content:encoded:
So as most of you know, I write Dread & Circuses, and I normally portray the Circus as a sanctuary for Anomalous Humanoids. Here I wanted to try something different: write an Anomalous Humanoid who's happy he escaped the Circus and ended up in containment.

Anyway, with this SCP I have now achieved my arbitrary goal of having an SCP in every century of series IV. Series V really kind of snuck up on us didn't it?

As usual, thanks to my Beta Readers; Zyn, Lazar, stormbreath, V-something, Floppy Phoenix, Dog Teeth, among others.

P.S. It's intentionally ambiguous whether the Clown with Manny is Icky or Lolly. Whichever you prefer is fine.
